title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (5)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, legal chronology, jewellery inventory, financial figures, and paragraph order. Names, dates, weights, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Allegations and findings remain attributed to the judgment and witnesses described in the letter. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. Page images 130-138 were used to restore the opening salutation, remove the stray `150)` page-number artifact, recover the printed `16-5-2005` adjournment date, and repair damaged symbols, numerals, proper names, and mechanical OCR joins. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: I thought I could summarise Judge Cunha's judgment for you in one or two letters to my Udanpirappu. Even after describing it through four letters, however, I have not reached the end. I now intend to conclude with another one or two letters.

From what I have explained so far, you will have understood the truth about Jayalalithaa's accumulation of assets. You must help the people of the country understand what you have understood. It is natural for mothers to feel sympathy when a woman is sentenced to prison. But neither the Judge nor we inflicted any harm upon the accused. Every adverse consequence was brought upon them by their own conduct. No one else can be blamed. We must make that truth understood.

I also wish to point out that this long series was prompted by the advertisements and statements attacking us: from AIADMK office-holders who believed that only page after page of advertisements would preserve their positions; from those removed from office who hoped to regain them; and from those yearning for some post through which they might increase their comforts and opportunities.
The accused obtained adjournments 130 times from 2010 alone, which shows the lengths to which they went to delay and prolong the trial.

Consider another example. Just when judgment was approaching in Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case, five companies suddenly filed separate petitions in the Special Court in March. They claimed that their properties had been included in the case and sought release from it. Judge Cunha dismissed their petitions on 14 March 2014, condemned their attempt to stay the principal case and fined them Rs. 10,000 for wasting the Court's time.

As usual, Lex Properties appealed to the Karnataka High Court. In his judgment of 29 April 2014, Justice Sathyanarayana dismissed the petition. He held that the Rs. 10,000 fine imposed by the Special Court was too low for conduct that further wasted judicial time, increased it tenfold to Rs. 1 lakh and ordered payment within fifteen days.

Lex Properties appealed to the Supreme Court. On 8 May 2014, Justices Chauhan, Chelameswar and Iqbal rejected its request to stay the disproportionate-assets trial and condemned the company for obstructing the proceedings.

Like Lex Properties, Meadow Agro Farm and Riverway Agro Farm filed separate petitions seeking an inquiry into their applications to be released from the disproportionate-assets case and a stay of the principal trial in the meantime. Judge Cunha rejected the request for a stay and fined each company Rs. 5,000 for wasting the Court's time.

In responding to each argument advanced by Jayalalithaa's side in the Bengaluru Special Court, Judge John Michael D'Cunha held that the Governor had properly authorised the prosecution of Jayalalithaa and that there was nothing unlawful in assigning Nallamma Naidu to investigate the case.

Judge Cunha also stated: “Although Jayalalithaa's counsel argued that statements had been used with malicious intent, he could not challenge even one statement during the trial in a manner that supported his allegation.”

After the case was transferred to Karnataka, Jayalalithaa petitioned the Karnataka High Court in Bengaluru to quash the entire criminal proceedings initiated against her under the Chennai Special Judge's order of 5 June 1997. The Karnataka High Court dismissed that petition on 10 March 2010. The Supreme Court dismissed her appeal on 19 March 2010. Judge Cunha therefore rejected the argument that the criminal proceedings against Jayalalithaa's side were unlawful.

It was not Judge Cunha alone. When the disproportionate-assets trial against Jayalalithaa and the other three began in Bengaluru on 28 March 2005, two years after the transfer, Jayalalithaa did not appear in the Special Court. Her side sought a month's adjournment. Special Judge Pachapure refused and adjourned the case to 18 April 2005.

Neither Jayalalithaa nor Sasikala appeared on the eighteenth. Jayalalithaa's lawyer was absent too. An assistant to senior counsel for the accused said that the senior lawyer was unwell and in hospital. The Judge warned sternly: “Let the lawyer remain absent. Where are the four accused? I will deliver judgment on the basis of their failure to appear.”

The Judge adjourned the trial to 9 May 2005 and ordered Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and the others to appear without fail. When the case was heard on 9 May, senior counsel K.T. Tulsi sought a lengthy adjournment for Jayalalithaa's side. Judge Pachapure became angry and said: “I grant a final adjournment until the sixteenth. I will grant no further time.” He ordered all four, including Jayalalithaa and Sasikala, to appear without fail that day.
When their lawyers again sought an adjournment, do you know what Judge Pachapure said?

“A request for a long adjournment cannot be accepted. I intend to conduct the case from day to day. How can the trial proceed quickly if adjournments are repeatedly requested? Is it right to delay the case for two and a half years through adjournments and now seek another one? The Supreme Court has directed me to conduct this case expeditiously. No hearing has taken place in the past six months. I have sat alone in this Court for six months. I feel as though I have been confined in solitary imprisonment.”
Judge Pachapure spoke then in such anguish.
Udanpirappē, these details show how Jayalalithaa's side used a different tactic at every stage to delay the proceedings and turn the judicial process into a spectacle.

After Nallamma Naidu, the Investigating Officer in Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case, retired, DSP Sambandam was placed in charge. Forgetting his legal duty, Sambandam appeared and testified in support of the accused. Examining an Investigating Officer as a defence witness in the very case he investigated violates Indian criminal procedure. Even this minimum legal requirement was not followed.

DSP Sambandam was secretly brought to court as the final witness, the ninety-ninth, and made to testify. Though an officer of the Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption, he buried the basic duty he owed as a government official, was examined as a defence witness and was allowed to cite evidentiary documents in support of the accused.

The same officer had earlier bypassed the Public Prosecutor and written directly to the Special Judge seeking reinvestigation of the case. The Karnataka High Court accepted the complaint we filed, rejected DSP Sambandam's request and condemned him as an officer acting under the orders of the then Tamil Nadu Chief Minister, Jayalalithaa.
Udanpirappē, this too shows how, once they regained power in Tamil Nadu, Jayalalithaa's side openly engaged in unlawful measures to divert the trial.

Now consider the jewellery accumulated by Jayalalithaa's side. Gold weighing 23 kilograms and 113 grams was seized from Jayalalithaa's residence. Its value at the time was Rs. 91,57,253. The diamonds in her residence were valued at Rs. 2,43,92,790. The total value of the gold and diamond jewellery was Rs. 3,35,50,043.

In addition, a search at the Poes Garden house yielded forty-two jewellery boxes containing ornaments. Some boxes held wristwatches; the others contained 131 pieces of jewellery weighing 4 kilograms and 475 grams. Their contemporary value was Rs. 17,37,266. Gold earrings set with diamonds were valued at Rs. 47,61,816.
Jayalalithaa's counsel P. Kumar argued that jewellery listed by the prosecution under serial numbers 284 and 285 had belonged to Jayalalithaa before she became Chief Minister.

Jayalalithaa filed together in 1992 her income-tax returns for 1987 and the following four years. On 26 March 1993, she paid the tax for 1992. There was a very large difference between the returns filed for successive years. When the Income Tax Department questioned it, they claimed that the value of gold and diamond jewellery had multiplied within a single year and that they had received many gifts during that period.

The Assistant Commissioner of Income Tax then issued a notice stating: “You have said that you received nineteen new ornaments as gifts during the previous year. They weighed 7,040 grams, and you also received diamonds weighing 101.49 carats. But you have not properly itemised them.” This evidence was clearly submitted to the Court. Judge Cunha explained that it proved the gifts and new jewellery arrived only after 1992.
Judge Cunha further said:

“What this shows is that jewellery accumulated after 1991 was backdated in the accounts and included in income-tax returns. Merely paying tax does not make that income lawfully earned. As a public servant, Jayalalithaa had to explain acceptably how the income arose. Yet she could never explain how kilograms of gold and diamonds entered her house.

“Jayalalithaa's counsel reminded the Court that all these items stood in Sasikala's name. If so, how did Sasikala acquire so much jewellery? Should she not at least prove that? She could not say how or when she purchased it. The evidence proves that Jayalalithaa purchased and accumulated the gold and diamonds in Sasikala's name. Neither Jayalalithaa nor Sasikala produced evidence to refute this. The charge is therefore proved.”
He continued:

“The jewellery seized from Jayalalithaa weighed 27,588 grams. Defence evidence established that she possessed 7,040 grams before taking office, so I exclude that amount from the case. The remaining 20,548 grams of gold must therefore have been acquired during her term in office.

“The Court has decided to value it at the 1992 gold price accepted by both sides. This produces a value of Rs. 89,05,503. Jayalalithaa's diamonds were valued at Rs. 1,62,61,820. Added to the gold jewellery, the total comes to Rs. 2,51,59,144. The Court declares that all jewellery made from gold and diamonds of this value was acquired by Jayalalithaa through improper means.”
Judge Cunha explained this in his lengthy judgment.

Sasikala filed her statement of assets for 1990-91 in 1993. She declared gold and diamond jewellery valued at Rs. 12,95,704 and five kilograms of silver articles valued at Rs. 27,468. But when she filed her statement for 1991-92, she declared gold and diamond jewellery worth Rs. 1,10,13,946 and silver articles worth Rs. 70,61,400.

In other words, jewellery worth Rs. 12.95 lakh in 1990-91 became jewellery worth Rs. 1.10 crore in 1991-92, the very next year. Does that not establish the speed at which the accumulation of assets proceeded?
Udanpirappē, on page 900 of the judgment, Judge Cunha wrote:

> “Heady mix of power and wealth is the bottom line in this case. Huge accumulation of wealth by accused in a short span of five years is a telling example how power would lead to concentration of unlawful wealth posing veritable danger to the democratic structure.”

That is to say: the foundation of this case is the authority the accused possessed and the wealth accumulated through it. Their accumulation of enormous property within only five years is itself a compelling example of how the use of power to concentrate unlawful wealth can endanger the structure of democracy.
Does not Judge Cunha's observation reveal the extent to which the assets accumulated by Jayalalithaa's side could damage the democratic order?
I have set out the details of the jewellery they accumulated. The judgment contains several more important matters. I shall continue with them tomorrow.
With affection,
M.K.
25 October 2014
